item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent brain-breaking sci-fi, wedded to just enough of a human touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SCP wiki can be divided into two classes:

  1. People who have upvoted SCP-5552
  2. People who haven't read SCP-5552

item.title: A masterpiece.
item.content:encoded: The best Series VI article, if not the best article on-site, period. Absolutely mindblowingly phenomenal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mindblowing. Recommended this after hitting a block on my own draft, and I can't believe I've been sleeping on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had some real strong Steins;Gate vibes at:

+ Collapsing to avoid S;G spoilers
– hide block

Helsman: You… you've already used it, haven't you?

I thought of Kurisu realising Okabe had used the time leap machine, and:

What if the only way to stop it, was to stop us from inventing time travel in the first place?

The True End is essentially ensuring the time machine is never made / is unmade.

The ending gave me some very similar feelings as well.

It worked extremely well in this context, and felt like a subtle nod towards Steins;Gate rather than being derivative of it. Excellent work (as someone who is currently plotting out his own vaguely S;G inspired piece of fiction, you may have sparked some new ideas for me to play around with, so thanks for that :) ).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: time travel fiction done right! this is some good shit. I'd upvote ten times if i could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great and I love it but I question why "Wilt" is capitalized if it is just a property of an atom like charge or spin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The science hooked me but I stayed for the feels. You made me log in for the first time in a while just to upvote this thing.

The part about Gupta's, Helsman's, and Green's different areas of expertise especially rang true. Research really is often a team effort, and very few people can know in enough depth the varying component areas of a project. It was very realistic that by themselves they couldn't figure it out (and also the interpersonal/human consequences that had). In addition, the ambiguity, the groping for answers, the rejected hypotheses and the problem-solving process, the Foundation not having all the answers, etc. were another authentic touch. Even though the technobabble wasn't like Elementary Time Travel levels of impressive verisimilitude and elaboration, you got the experience of science right more than most articles.

A few other random thoughts: I liked how understated/backgrounded the emotional part was to the main action, I thought that actually made it more powerful (and realistic given the setting/characters). I got such "Kimi no na wa" vibes from the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best science fiction stories I've had the pleasure of reading. Well done!

item.title: Awesome!
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for this, it's a very good and well-written article. There are not that many hard science fiction articles here and this is a great contribution.

I also love the image you used, normally people would have used a tunnel-like wormhole.

Great job! Thanks again, I've enjoyed this very much :)
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was such a cathartic piece to read. Dr. Gupta and Dr. Helsmann's hubris clouded their judgment, and they ended up screwing everything up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You fool, this skip has always been cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "but did you like SCP-5552 before it was cool."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you can if you downvote then upvote

item.title: Wow just wow
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely on of the best SCP entries I have ever read. It definitely competes with articles like SCP 3001. I'm still new to the SCP community but anyone can see the quality and effort that went into this post. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With a lot of info-dump SCP's similar to this one, I lose the plot entirely and end up having to re-read entire paragraphs before I give up. I don't know how, but this didn't happen once while reading 5552. Everything just flowed so damn well.

+1
Aaaay! I'm really happy so many people have enjoyed this piece. Thank you all for giving it the time of day!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm crying Alan. Why am I crying?

Ow, this hurt.

This was an amazing piece. The utilization of time travel intermixed with the character interactions was totally heart-wrenching. I'll refrain from stating anymore, since I believe it's all been said already, but absolutely gorgeous piece.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best articles I have ever read I wasn’t bored for a second and I NEEDED to see what the next document was there was nothing that felt off the writing just flowed perfectly this is a masterpiece +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Top-notch science fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I considered keeping it to myself and trying to publish it as traditional fiction somehow. But I realized that it just worked too well in the SCP format, and trying to replicate this in traditional prose probably would've lost some of it's punchiness, since the format lets me get away with leaving out a lot since readers sort of fill in the gaps themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An instant classic for sure. Goddamn this was unreasonably excellent. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing. My favourite SCP is 2998, and this one here feels incredibly similar. I love how Gupta realizes he needed Cindy for the machine to work, and how, after the "reset", Cindy keeps a sort of unconscious memory of Gupta and she starts crying. It's an interesting concept that I haven't seen used in any time travel story: what happens when someone travels to the past too many times in a row.

I wish I could upvote more than once. I'd give this a +10 or +100 if I could!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope 5552 becomes as instantly recognizable as 3001.
Hooray! It's been a minute since a non-contest piece of mine has shot up this fast. I'm glad you've all enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'm too stupid to fully understand the entire plot, but I thoroughly enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely going to be one of those articles that is seen as a classic a couple of years from now. Really well done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, we never learn if the machine is an actual time machine or a multiverse machine.

This is very much on purpose. Although if it's that important to you, you can try to come up with an answer yourself. I don't have one, but that don't let that stop you from coming up with an answer for yourself.

I'm glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of pretty neat details and the pacing is done well enough to make the standard "try to fix time travel related mistakes" story compelling. The part that is a stand-out to me is the character interactions since I found them to be emotional enough without getting hammy, which is a pitfall I find a lot of similar stories fall into. The last interview demonstrates how being concise and realistic is much more effective than having characters monologue or use flowery language to basically hit the reader in the face with what they're supposed to feel.

The very well done technobabble, sci-fi elements, and pacing are what hold the story in place and makes it work (and stand out from other stories with a similar premise), but it's these sentimental aspects that elevate it. The bittersweet ending is also very much the perfect spot to end it, even though you really made me want a happy ending.

+1 this was a very fun ride.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article.

The attention to detail, the descriptions, the emotion in it all is just so wonderful.

However, we never learn if the machine is an actual time machine or a multiverse machine.

But still, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Masterful. This is the sort of article that transcends this site and feels like "proper" SF. Like, it's the kind of thing I would expect to read in a collection of short stories from Asimov and Heinlein and the other greats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will not lie, there was definitely some Steins;Gate inspiration at play here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Impressive, both conception and execution.
As someone who absolutely loves Steins;Gate and Doctor Who, this was a field day. Very clever.

It's always excellent when a scip can go beyond just the format and make the SCP-entry itself something extraordinary.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely wonderful piece! Interesting premise, emotionality that doesn't feel forced, and such clean writing.

Take my +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now the question is… when will you write something to make me angry? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is the kind of article where, some years down the line, i'll brag that i was around when it was written. this feels like a milestone article for the site; the subtle beauty and extremely human nature of it is nearly unparalleled. thank you for making this :>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bloody excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely phenomenal. I tend to enjoy most SCPs I read, but this really, truly raised the bar for pretty much everything I read going forward. Incredibly job, CaptainKirby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: damn. impressive. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the best piece of content I’ve read on the site since 3001. It’s absolutely outstanding. A high-concept Sci-Fi premise exploring a story which is only too human. Every emotional beat hits the mark and there isn’t a thing in it that’s not earned. This transcends the format and is a legitimately immersive, engrossing and heart-wrenching story of hubris, ego, and the human condition.

I don’t want to go into too much detail because of the possibility of spoiling the fun for others. But if you read an SCP this year, you’re doing yourself a disservice if you’re not reading this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely an amazing read! Wish I could upvote twice!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, that means a lot <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is unacceptably good. there's just so much going on here, with the characters, the introduction of the paratech, the use of unknowns, the hints about the varying universes with the headers

I'm almost angry how quality this all is. Kirby, you bastard.

item.title: Hey you, seeing this in New Posts
item.content:encoded: You should give this a read! It's reeeeeeeeeeeeeeaaaaaaaaaal good!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God this is amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Legitimately one of the best things I've ever read on the SCP wiki, period. I don't say that lightly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A masterpiece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this shit is obscenely goddamn good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Get used to what's coming now
Is it all way too familiar
Is it what you want, you want?

I'd like to thank Calibri_Bold, Cerastes, aismallard, AbsentmindedNihilist, Gee0765, Oboebandgeek99 UraniumEmpire, Tufto and Rounderhouse for looking at this as a draft.
